concepts.name: euclidean distance
concepts.description: Standard distance metric ||x-x'|| used in k-nearest neighbors.

concepts.name: responsibility
concepts.description: In the EM algorithm for Gaussian mixtures, the weight assigned to each observation for each cluster based on the likelihood of the corresponding Gaussians.

concepts.name: model-free methods
concepts.description: Simple, highly unstructured methods for classification and pattern recognition that are not useful for understanding relationships but can be very effective as black box prediction engines.

concepts.name: learning vector quantization
concepts.description: An online algorithm due to Kohonen where prototypes are placed strategically with respect to decision boundaries, with training points attracting prototypes of the correct class and repelling others.

concepts.name: soft clustering
concepts.description: Clustering method where observations are assigned fractional responsibilities or weights for each cluster, as in Gaussian mixture models.

concepts.name: hard clustering
concepts.description: Clustering method where observations are assigned to exactly one cluster, as in K-means.

concepts.name: soft classification
concepts.description: Classification approach that produces smooth posterior probabilities for each class, though the final decision rule still selects the class with maximum probability.

concepts.name: Cover and Hart result
concepts.description: A famous result showing that asymptotically the error rate of the 1-nearest-neighbor classifier is never more than twice the Bayes rate.

concepts.name: adaptive nearest-neighbor
concepts.description: Nearest-neighbor rules designed to alleviate the substantial bias that can occur in real problems with the standard nearest-neighbor approach.

concepts.name: Algorithm 14.1 K-means Clustering
concepts.description:
1. For a given cluster assignment C, the total cluster variance (14.33) is
minimized with respect to {m1, . . . , mK} yielding the means of the
currently assigned clusters (14.32).

concepts.name: Voronoi tessellation
concepts.description:
This can be minimized by an alternating optimization procedure given in
Algorithm 14.1.
Each of steps 1 and 2 reduces the value of the criterion (14.33), so that
convergence is assured. However, the result may represent a suboptimal
local minimum. The algorithm of Hartigan and Wong (1979) goes further,
and ensures that there is no single switch of an observation from one group
to another group that will decrease the objective. In addition, one should
start the algorithm with many diﬀerent random choices for the starting
means, and choose the solution having smallest value of the objective func-
tion.
Figure 14.6 shows some of the K-means iterations for the simulated data
of Figure 14.4. The centroids are depicted by “O”s. The straight lines show
the partitioning of points, each sector being the set of points closest to
each centroid. This partitioning is called the Voronoi tessellation. After 20
iterations the procedure has converged.

concepts.name: codebook
concepts.description:
numbers is regarded as a vector in IR4. A K-means clustering algorithm
(also known as Lloyd’s algorithm in this context) is run in this space.
The center image uses K = 200, while the right image K = 4. Each of
the 512 × 512 pixel blocks (or points) is approximated by its closest cluster
centroid, known as a codeword. The clustering process is called the encoding
step, and the collection of centroids is called the codebook.
To represent the approximated image, we need to supply for each block
the identity of the codebook entry that approximates it. This will require
log2(K) bits per block. We also need to supply the codebook itself, which
is K × 4 real numbers (typically negligible). Overall, the storage for the
compressed image amounts to log2(K)/(4 · 8) of the original (0.239 for
K = 200, 0.063 for K = 4). This is typically expressed as a rate in bits
per pixel: log2(K)/4, which are 1.91 and 0.50, respectively. The process
of constructing the approximate image from the centroids is called the
decoding step.
Why do we expect VQ to work at all? The reason is that for typical
everyday images like photographs, many of the blocks look the same. In
this case there are many almost pure white blocks, and similarly pure gray
blocks of various shades. These require only one block each to represent
them, and then multiple pointers to that block.
What we have described is known as lossy compression, since our im-
ages are degraded versions of the original. The degradation or distortion is
usually measured in terms of mean squared error. In this case D = 0.89
for K = 200 and D = 16.95 for K = 4. More generally a rate/distortion
curve would be used to assess the tradeoﬀ. One can also perform lossless
compression using block clustering, and still capitalize on the repeated pat-
terns. If you took the original image and losslessly compressed it, the best
you would do is 4.48 bits per pixel.
We claimed above that log2(K) bits were needed to identify each of the K
codewords in the codebook. This uses a ﬁxed-length code, and is ineﬃcient
if some codewords occur many more times than others in the image. Using
Shannon coding theory, we know that in general a variable length code
will do better, and the rate then becomes −	K
ℓ=1 pℓlog2(pℓ)/4. The term
in the numerator is the entropy of the distribution pℓof the codewords
in the image. Using variable length coding our rates come down to 1.42
and 0.39, respectively. Finally, there are many generalizations of VQ that
have been developed: for example, tree-structured VQ ﬁnds the centroids
with a top-down, 2-means style algorithm, as alluded to in Section 14.3.12.
This allows successive reﬁnement of the compression. Further details may
be found in Gersho and Gray (1992).

concepts.description:

ℓ=1 pℓlog2(pℓ)/4. The term
in the numerator is the entropy of the distribution pℓof the codewords
in the image. Using variable length coding our rates come down to 1.42
and 0.39, respectively. Finally, there are many generalizations of VQ that

This allows successive reﬁnement of the compression. Further details may
be found in Gersho and Gray (1992).

concepts.name: lossy compression
concepts.description:
numbers is regarded as a vector in IR4. A K-means clustering algorithm
(also known as Lloyd’s algorithm in this context) is run in this space.
The center image uses K = 200, while the right image K = 4. Each of

ℓ=1 pℓlog2(pℓ)/4. The term
in the numerator is the entropy of the distribution pℓof the codewords

be found in Gersho and Gray (1992).

concepts.name: chaining
concepts.description:
observations within them are relatively close together (small dissimilarities)
as compared with observations in diﬀerent clusters. To the extent this is
not the case, results will diﬀer.
Single linkage (14.41) only requires that a single dissimilarity dii′, i ∈G
and i′ ∈H, be small for two groups G and H to be considered close
together, irrespective of the other observation dissimilarities between the
groups. It will therefore have a tendency to combine, at relatively low
thresholds, observations linked by a series of close intermediate observa-
tions. This phenomenon, referred to as chaining, is often considered a de-
fect of the method. The clusters produced by single linkage can violate the
“compactness” property that all observations within each cluster tend to
be similar to one another, based on the supplied observation dissimilari-
ties {dii′}. If we deﬁne the diameter DG of a group of observations as the
largest dissimilarity among its members

concepts.name: left singular vectors
concepts.description:
This is a standard decomposition in numerical analysis, and many algo-
rithms exist for its computation (Golub and Van Loan, 1983, for example).
Here U is an N × p orthogonal matrix (UT U = Ip) whose columns uj are
called the left singular vectors; V is a p×p orthogonal matrix (VT V = Ip)
with columns vj called the right singular vectors, and D is a p×p diagonal
matrix, with diagonal elements d1 ≥d2 ≥· · · ≥dp ≥0 known as the sin-
gular values. For each rank q, the solution Vq to (14.53) consists of the ﬁrst
q columns of V. The columns of UD are called the principal components
of X (see Section 3.5.1). The N optimal ˆλi in (14.52) are given by the ﬁrst
q principal components (the N rows of the N × q matrix UqDq).
The one-dimensional principal component line in IR2 is illustrated in Fig-
ure 14.20. For each data point xi, there is a closest point on the line, given
by ui1d1v1. Here v1 is the direction of the line and ˆλi = ui1d1 measures
distance along the line from the origin. Similarly Figure 14.21 shows the

concepts.description:
This is a standard decomposition in numerical analysis, and many algo-
rithms exist for its computation (Golub and Van Loan, 1983, for example).
Here U is an N × p orthogonal matrix (UT U = Ip) whose columns uj are
called the left singular vectors; V is a p×p orthogonal matrix (VT V = Ip)
with columns vj called the right singular vectors, and D is a p×p diagonal
matrix, with diagonal elements d1 ≥d2 ≥· · · ≥dp ≥0 known as the sin-
gular values. For each rank q, the solution Vq to (14.53) consists of the ﬁrst
q columns of V. The columns of UD are called the principal components
of X (see Section 3.5.1). The N optimal ˆλi in (14.52) are given by the ﬁrst
q principal components (the N rows of the N × q matrix UqDq).
The one-dimensional principal component line in IR2 is illustrated in Fig-
ure 14.20. For each data point xi, there is a closest point on the line, given
by ui1d1v1. Here v1 is the direction of the line and ˆλi = ui1d1 measures

concepts.name: Procrustes distance
concepts.description:
and the minimal distances is referred to as the Procrustes distance. From
the form of the solution, we can center each matrix at its column centroid,
and then ignore location completely. Hereafter we assume this is the case.
The Procrustes distance with scaling solves a slightly more general
problem,

concepts.name: principal points of the distribution
concepts.description:
interested mainly in the smooth ones. A principal curve is illustrated in
Figure 14.27.
Principal points are an interesting related concept. Consider a set of k
prototypes and for each point x in the support of a distribution, identify
the closest prototype, that is, the prototype that is responsible for it. This
induces a partition of the feature space into so-called Voronoi regions. The
set of k points that minimize the expected distance from X to its prototype
are called the principal points of the distribution. Each principal point is
self-consistent, in that it equals the mean of X in its Voronoi region. For
example, with k = 1, the principal point of a circular normal distribution is
the mean vector; with k = 2 they are a pair of points symmetrically placed
on a ray through the mean vector. Principal points are the distributional
analogs of centroids found by K-means clustering. Principal curves can be
viewed as k = ∞principal points, but constrained to lie on a smooth curve,
in a similar way that a SOM constrains K-means cluster centers to fall on
a smooth manifold.
To ﬁnd a principal curve f(λ) of a distribution, we consider its coordinate
functions f(λ) = [f1(λ), f2(λ), . . . , fp(λ)] and let XT = (X1, X2, . . . , Xp).
Consider the following alternating steps:

concepts.name: adjacency matrix
concepts.description:
Alternatively, a fully connected graph includes all pairwise edges with
weights wii′ = sii′, and the local behavior is controlled by the scale param-
eter c.
The matrix of edge weights W = {wii′} from a similarity graph is called
the adjacency matrix. The degree of vertex i is gi = 	
i′ wii′, the sum of
the weights of the edges connected to it. Let G be a diagonal matrix with
diagonal elements gi.
Finally, the graph Laplacian is deﬁned by

concepts.name: unnormalized graph Laplacian
concepts.description:
This is called the unnormalized graph Laplacian; a number of normalized
versions have been proposed—these standardize the Laplacian with respect
to the node degrees gi, for example, ˜L = I −G−1W.
Spectral clustering ﬁnds the m eigenvectors ZN×m corresponding to the
m smallest eigenvalues of L (ignoring the trivial constant eigenvector).
Using a standard method like K-means, we then cluster the rows of Z to
yield a clustering of the original data points.
An example is presented in Figure 14.29. The top left panel shows 450
simulated data points in three circular clusters indicated by the colors. K-
means clustering would clearly have diﬃculty identifying the outer clusters.
We applied spectral clustering using a 10-nearest neighbor similarity graph,
and display the eigenvector corresponding to the second and third smallest
eigenvalue of the graph Laplacian in the lower left. The 15 smallest eigen-
values are shown in the top right panel. The two eigenvectors shown have
identiﬁed the three clusters, and a scatterplot of the rows of the eigenvector
matrix Y in the bottom right clearly separates the clusters. A procedure
such as K-means clustering applied to these transformed points would eas-
ily identify the three groups.
Why does spectral clustering work? For any vector f we have

concepts.name: factor loadings
concepts.description:
where Dε = diag[Var(ε1), . . . , Var(εp)]. The Sj being Gaussian and un-
correlated makes them statistically independent random variables. Thus a
battery of educational test scores would be thought to be driven by the
independent underlying factors such as intelligence, drive and so on. The
columns of A are referred to as the factor loadings, and are used to name
and interpret the factors.

concepts.name: Local MDS
concepts.description:
FIGURE 14.44. The orange points show data lying on a parabola, while the blue
points shows multidimensional scaling representations in one dimension. Classical
multidimensional scaling (left panel) does not preserve the ordering of the points
along the curve, because it judges points on opposite ends of the curve to be close
together. In contrast, local multidimensional scaling (right panel) does a good job
of preserving the ordering of the points along the curve.

concepts.name: singular value decomposition
concepts.description: A decomposition X = UDV^T where U and V are orthogonal matrices and D is diagonal, providing insight into variance structure of inputs.

concepts.name: reproducing kernel hilbert space
concepts.description: A mathematical framework (RKHS) for function estimation with kernels, providing theoretical foundation for support vector machines.

concepts.name: prototype methods
concepts.description: Classification methods that represent training data by a set of points in feature space, where each prototype has an associated class label and classification is made to the class of the closest prototype.

concepts.name: k-means clustering for classification
concepts.description: Application of K-means clustering to labeled data by applying K-means separately to training data in each class, using R prototypes per class.

concepts.name: within cluster variance
concepts.description: The total variance within clusters that K-means procedure iteratively minimizes by moving cluster centers.

concepts.name: LVQ1
concepts.description: The basic learning vector quantization procedure that moves prototypes towards training points of the same class and away from training points of different classes.

concepts.name: LVQ2
concepts.description: A modification of LVQ1 that can sometimes improve performance.

concepts.name: LVQ3
concepts.description: A modification of LVQ1 that can sometimes improve performance.

concepts.name: online algorithm
concepts.description: An algorithm where observations are processed one at a time, as in LVQ.

concepts.name: stochastic approximation learning rates
concepts.description: Guidelines for decreasing the learning rate to zero with each iteration in online learning algorithms like LVQ.

concepts.name: k-nearest-neighbor classifier
concepts.description: A memory-based classifier that finds k training points closest to a query point and classifies using majority vote among the k neighbors.

concepts.name: memory-based classifier
concepts.description: A classifier that requires no model to be fit and uses the training data directly for prediction.

concepts.name: majority vote
concepts.description: A classification decision rule where the class is chosen based on the most common class among k nearest neighbors.

concepts.name: asymptotic nearest-neighbor error bound
concepts.description: The property that 1-nearest-neighbor error rate is bounded by twice the Bayes error rate asymptotically.

concepts.name: discriminant adaptive nearest-neighbor
concepts.description: An adaptive nearest-neighbor method that uses a local linear discriminant analysis to estimate the local between-class covariance matrix and adaptively shape the neighborhood.

concepts.name: chi-square distance
concepts.description: A distance measure for discrete distributions used in analyzing market basket data and contingency tables.

concepts.name: market basket analysis
concepts.description: Analysis of large databases of customer transactions to discover association rules showing which items are purchased together.

concepts.name: association rules
concepts.description: Rules of the form X => Y indicating that when itemset X appears in a transaction, itemset Y is also likely to appear, measured by support and confidence.

concepts.name: support
concepts.description: In association rules, the percentage of transactions containing both itemsets X and Y in the rule X => Y.

concepts.name: confidence
concepts.description: In association rules, the percentage of transactions containing X that also contain Y in the rule X => Y.

concepts.name: apriori algorithm
concepts.description: An efficient algorithm for finding frequent itemsets in market basket analysis by exploiting the downward closure property.

concepts.name: downward closure property
concepts.description: The property that if an itemset is frequent, then all of its subsets must also be frequent, exploited by the Apriori algorithm.

concepts.name: frequent itemset
concepts.description: An itemset that appears in at least a minimum support threshold of transactions in market basket analysis.

concepts.name: lift
concepts.description: A measure for association rules that compares the confidence to the baseline frequency of the consequent, indicating whether the rule provides improvement over random chance.

concepts.name: generalized association rules
concepts.description: Extension of association rules to include quantitative and categorical features beyond binary transactions, often by discretization.

concepts.name: unsupervised as supervised learning
concepts.description: A framework for unsupervised learning problems like clustering and association rules where a supervised learning model is used to predict the presence or absence of features.

concepts.name: cluster analysis
concepts.description: Methods for finding groups or clusters in data such that observations within each group are more similar to each other than to observations in other groups.

concepts.name: data segmentation
concepts.description: Another name for cluster analysis, the process of grouping data into meaningful subsets.

concepts.name: dissimilarity
concepts.description: A measure of how different two observations are, typically satisfying d(xi, xi) = 0 and d(xi, xi′) ≥ 0.

concepts.name: attribute dissimilarity
concepts.description: Dissimilarity measures based on the attributes or features of observations, such as Euclidean distance for quantitative variables.

concepts.name: gower dissimilarity
concepts.description: A general dissimilarity coefficient for mixed data types (quantitative, ordinal, and categorical) that combines different attribute-specific dissimilarities.

concepts.name: object dissimilarity
concepts.description: Dissimilarity measures that treat observations as unstructured objects without explicit attributes, measured directly between observations.

concepts.name: combinatorial clustering algorithms
concepts.description: Clustering algorithms that work directly with observed data or dissimilarities and assign observations to clusters.

concepts.name: mixture modeling clustering
concepts.description: A probabilistic clustering approach that assumes data is generated from a mixture of underlying probability distributions.

concepts.name: mode seeking clustering
concepts.description: Clustering methods that attempt to directly estimate distinct modes of the probability density function, with each mode representing a cluster.

concepts.name: hierarchical clustering
concepts.description: Clustering methods that produce a sequence of nested partitions from fine (all singletons) to coarse (all observations in one cluster), often represented as a dendrogram.

concepts.name: dendrogram
concepts.description: A tree diagram representing the hierarchical clustering structure showing how observations and clusters are merged or split at different levels.

concepts.name: agglomerative clustering
concepts.description: A bottom-up hierarchical clustering approach that starts with each observation as its own cluster and successively merges closest pairs.

concepts.name: divisive clustering
concepts.description: Top-down hierarchical clustering starting with all data in one cluster, recursively splitting clusters to maximize between-group dissimilarity.

concepts.name: single linkage
concepts.description: A dissimilarity measure between two groups defined as the minimum dissimilarity between any observation in one group and any observation in the other.

concepts.name: complete linkage
concepts.description: A dissimilarity measure between two groups defined as the maximum dissimilarity between any observation in one group and any observation in the other.

concepts.name: average linkage
concepts.description: A dissimilarity measure between two groups defined as the average dissimilarity between all pairs of observations from the two groups.

concepts.name: Ward's method
concepts.description: An agglomerative hierarchical clustering method that minimizes the within-cluster sum of squares at each merge, similar to K-means objective.

concepts.name: vector quantization
concepts.description: Image and signal compression technique using K-means clustering to approximate blocks of pixels by cluster centroids (codewords), with applications in lossy compression.

concepts.name: codeword
concepts.description: The cluster centroid used to approximate a block of pixels in vector quantization; the collection of codewords forms the codebook.

concepts.name: encoding step
concepts.description: In vector quantization, the clustering process that assigns each data block to its nearest codeword.

concepts.name: rate-distortion tradeoff
concepts.description: In lossy compression, the tradeoff between compression rate (bits per pixel) and distortion (reconstruction error).

concepts.name: variable length coding
concepts.description: A coding scheme that assigns shorter codes to more frequent codewords, using entropy-based Shannon coding theory.

concepts.name: entropy of codeword distribution
concepts.description: The Shannon entropy of the distribution of codewords in variable length coding, determining the optimal compression rate.

concepts.name: tree-structured vector quantization
concepts.description: A generalization of VQ that finds centroids with a top-down, 2-means style algorithm, allowing successive refinement of compression.

concepts.name: k-medoids clustering
concepts.description: Clustering method restricting cluster centers to actual observations, minimizing total dissimilarity to cluster members using arbitrary dissimilarity measures.

concepts.name: silhouette coefficient
concepts.description: A measure of clustering quality that compares within-cluster tightness to between-cluster separation for each observation.

concepts.name: gap statistic
concepts.description: Method for estimating optimal number of clusters by comparing log WK curve to that from uniformly distributed data, finding where gap is largest.

concepts.name: self-organizing map
concepts.description: A constrained version of K-means clustering where prototypes lie in a low-dimensional manifold (usually 1D or 2D grid), preserving topological relationships.

concepts.name: principal curves
concepts.description: Smooth one-dimensional curves that pass through the middle of a distribution, generalizing principal components to nonlinear manifolds.

concepts.name: principal surfaces
concepts.description: Smooth two-dimensional surfaces that generalize principal curves to higher-dimensional manifolds.

concepts.name: spectral clustering
concepts.description: A clustering method that uses the eigenvectors of the graph Laplacian matrix to embed data in a lower-dimensional space where clusters are more easily separated.

concepts.name: graph laplacian
concepts.description: A matrix representation of a graph defined as L = G - W where G is the diagonal degree matrix and W is the adjacency matrix, used in spectral clustering.

concepts.name: similarity graph
concepts.description: A graph representation of data where nodes are observations and edges represent similarities, used in spectral clustering.

concepts.name: kernel density estimation
concepts.description: A nonparametric method for estimating the probability density function by placing kernel functions at each data point.

concepts.name: encoding step in vector quantization
concepts.description: The clustering process in vector quantization where pixel blocks are assigned to their nearest codeword.

concepts.name: rate in bits per pixel
concepts.description: Measure of compression efficiency expressed as log2(K)/4 bits per pixel, where K is the number of codewords.

concepts.name: fixed-length code
concepts.description: Coding scheme using the same number of bits for each codeword, requiring log2(K) bits to identify K codewords.

concepts.name: variable length code
concepts.description: Coding scheme using different numbers of bits for different codewords based on frequency, achieving better compression via Shannon coding theory.

concepts.name: cophenetic correlation coefficient
concepts.description: Correlation between pairwise observation dissimilarities and their cophenetic dissimilarities from the dendrogram, measuring how well hierarchy represents data.

concepts.name: cophenetic dissimilarity
concepts.description: Intergroup dissimilarity at which two observations are first joined together in the same cluster in hierarchical clustering.

concepts.name: ultrametric inequality
concepts.description: Constraint on cophenetic dissimilarities requiring Cik ≤ max(Cii′, Ci′k) for any three observations, implying isosceles triangles.

concepts.name: single linkage agglomerative clustering
concepts.description: Hierarchical clustering taking intergroup dissimilarity as minimum dissimilarity between groups; tends to chain clusters via intermediate observations.

concepts.name: complete linkage agglomerative clustering
concepts.description: Hierarchical clustering taking intergroup dissimilarity as maximum dissimilarity between groups; produces compact clusters with small diameters.

concepts.name: group average clustering
concepts.description: Hierarchical clustering using average dissimilarity between groups; compromise between single and complete linkage, attempts compact and well-separated clusters.

concepts.name: cluster diameter
concepts.description: Largest dissimilarity among members of a cluster, measuring cluster compactness.

concepts.name: batch som
concepts.description: Batch version of self-organizing maps processing all observations together rather than one at a time.

concepts.name: neighborhood function in som
concepts.description: Function h giving more weight to prototypes closer in topological coordinate space, used in sophisticated SOM updates.

concepts.name: reconstruction error in clustering
concepts.description: Sum of squared distances ||x − mj||² between observations and their assigned prototypes, measuring clustering quality.

concepts.name: term-document matrix
concepts.description: Matrix representation where each row is a document and entries are relative frequencies of predefined terms, used for document retrieval.

concepts.name: principal component analysis
concepts.description: Sequence of best linear approximations to data of all ranks q ≤ p, providing uncorrelated projections ordered by variance.

concepts.name: rank-q linear model
concepts.description: Parametric representation of affine hyperplane f(λ) = μ + Vqλ approximating data points by least squares.

concepts.name: right singular vectors
concepts.description: Columns vj of matrix V in SVD, defining principal component directions; first q columns solve rank-q approximation problem.

concepts.name: singular values
concepts.description: Diagonal elements d1 ≥ d2 ≥ ⋅⋅⋅ ≥ dp ≥ 0 of diagonal matrix D in SVD, measuring variance along principal component directions.

concepts.name: procrustes transformation
concepts.description: Finding optimal rotation R and location μ to match two sets of corresponding points by minimizing ||X2 − X1R − 1μ^T||F.

concepts.name: procrustes average
concepts.description: Shape M minimizing average squared Procrustes distance to collection of L shapes, found by alternating algorithm.

concepts.name: principal curve
concepts.description: Smooth one-dimensional curved approximation to data satisfying self-consistency: f(λ) = E(X|λf(X) = λ), generalizing principal component line.

concepts.name: self-consistency property
concepts.description: Property of principal curves where f(λ) equals the average of all data points projecting to it.

concepts.name: principal surface
concepts.description: Higher-dimensional generalization of principal curves providing curved manifold approximation, commonly used in two dimensions.

concepts.name: mutual k-nearest-neighbor graph
concepts.description: Similarity graph connecting pairs where one point is among K-nearest neighbors of the other, with nonzero edge weights only for neighbors.

concepts.name: normalized graph laplacian
concepts.description: Standardized version of Laplacian with respect to node degrees, e.g., L̃ = I − G^(-1)W.

concepts.name: kernel principal components
concepts.description: Nonlinear PCA obtained by applying PCA in implicit feature space defined by kernel, expanding scope to non-linear transformations.

concepts.name: sparse principal components
concepts.description: PCA variant using L1 penalties to encourage sparse loadings, making principal components easier to interpret.

concepts.name: scotlass
concepts.description: Sparse PCA method maximizing variance subject to L1 constraint on loadings: max v^T(X^TX)v subject to ∑|vj| ≤ t, v^Tv = 1.

concepts.name: elastic net for sparse pca
concepts.description: Sparse PCA using regression/reconstruction property with ridge and lasso penalties, allowing efficient computation via alternating optimization.

concepts.name: non-negative matrix factorization
concepts.description: Alternative to PCA approximating X ≈ WH where all elements are non-negative, useful for modeling non-negative data like images.

concepts.name: archetypal analysis
concepts.description: Method approximating data points by convex combinations of prototypes (archetypes) that themselves are convex combinations of data, forcing prototypes onto convex hull.

concepts.name: factor analysis
concepts.description: Classical latent variable model X = AS + ε where S are uncorrelated factors, modeling correlation structure with separate disturbances ε.

concepts.name: independent component analysis
concepts.description: Latent variable model X = AS where components S are statistically independent (not just uncorrelated), exploiting non-Gaussianity for identifiability.

concepts.name: blind source separation
concepts.description: Recovery of independent source signals from mixed observations, classical application of ICA in cocktail party problem.

concepts.name: differential entropy
concepts.description: Entropy H(Y) = −∫g(y)log g(y)dy measuring randomness of continuous variable; Gaussian has maximum entropy for fixed variance.

concepts.name: mutual information
concepts.description: Kullback-Leibler distance between joint density and product of marginals, measuring dependence between components of random vector.

concepts.name: negentropy
concepts.description: Non-negative measure J(Yj) = H(Zj) − H(Yj) quantifying departure from Gaussianity, where Zj is Gaussian with same variance as Yj.

concepts.name: exploratory projection pursuit
concepts.description: Graphical exploration technique for visualizing high-dimensional data by finding non-Gaussian projections revealing interesting structure like clusters or tails.

concepts.name: projection indices
concepts.description: Measures focusing on different departures from Gaussianity for optimization in projection pursuit, similar to negentropy.

concepts.name: tilted gaussian density
concepts.description: Representation fj(sj) = φ(sj)e^(gj(sj)) for departures from Gaussianity, used in product density ICA approach.

concepts.name: prodeniica
concepts.description: ICA algorithm estimating product density directly using generalized additive models with penalized Poisson log-likelihood and smoothing splines.

concepts.name: amari metric
concepts.description: Measure of closeness between two frames (orthogonal mixing matrices), used to compare estimated and true mixing matrices in ICA.

concepts.name: multidimensional scaling
concepts.description: Finding low-dimensional representation zi ∈ R^k preserving pairwise dissimilarities dij, requiring only dissimilarities not data points.

concepts.name: kruskal-shephard scaling
concepts.description: Least squares MDS minimizing stress function SM = ∑i≠i′(dii′ − ||zi − zi′||)².

concepts.name: sammon mapping
concepts.description: MDS variation minimizing weighted stress putting more emphasis on preserving smaller pairwise distances.

concepts.name: classical scaling
concepts.description: MDS minimizing ∑i,i′(sii′ − ⟨zi, zi′⟩)² for similarities sii′, with explicit eigenvector solution; equivalent to PCA for centered inner products.

concepts.name: metric scaling
concepts.description: MDS methods (least squares and classical scaling) approximating actual dissimilarities or similarities rather than just ranks.

concepts.name: shephard-kruskal nonmetric scaling
concepts.description: MDS using only ranks, minimizing stress over zi and arbitrary increasing function θ via isotonic regression.

concepts.name: isotonic regression
concepts.description: Method finding best monotonic approximation θ(dii′) to ||zi − zi′|| with zi fixed in nonmetric scaling.

concepts.name: isometric feature mapping
concepts.description: Nonlinear dimension reduction constructing graph to approximate geodesic distance along manifold via shortest path, then applying classical scaling.

concepts.name: local linear embedding
concepts.description: Nonlinear dimension reduction preserving local affine structure by approximating points as linear combinations of neighbors in both high and low dimensions.

concepts.name: geodesic distance on manifold
concepts.description: Distance along manifold surface approximated by shortest path through neighbor graph in ISOMAP.

concepts.name: pagerank algorithm
concepts.description: An algorithm used by Google to rank webpages, considering a webpage important if many other important webpages point to it, with recursive weighting based on the PageRank of linking pages and their number of outgoing links.

concepts.description:

concepts.name: proximity matrix
concepts.description: An N × N matrix accumulated in random forests where for every tree, any pair of out-of-bag observations sharing a terminal node has their proximity increased by one, used to visualize similarity between observations.

concepts.name: random forest
concepts.description: A substantial modification of bagging that builds a large collection of de-correlated trees by randomly selecting a subset of variables at each split, and then averages them.

concepts.name: out-of-bag samples
concepts.description: A validation technique in random forests where each observation's prediction is made using only trees from bootstrap samples in which that observation did not appear, providing a built-in cross-validation estimate.

concepts.name: out-of-bag error
concepts.description: An error estimate in random forests that is almost identical to N-fold cross-validation, computed using out-of-bag samples.

concepts.name: variable importance via permutation
concepts.description: A method in random forests to measure variable importance by randomly permuting values of a variable in out-of-bag samples and measuring the decrease in prediction accuracy.

concepts.name: proximity plot
concepts.description: A two-dimensional visualization of the proximity matrix using multidimensional scaling, showing which observations are effectively close together according to the random forest classifier.

concepts.name: de-correlation effect in random forests
concepts.description: The variance reduction mechanism in random forests achieved by reducing correlation between trees through random selection of input variables at each split.

concepts.name: sampling correlation in random forests
concepts.description: The theoretical correlation ρ(x) between a pair of random-forest trees evaluated at x, induced by the sampling distribution of training data and randomization parameters.

concepts.name: within-z variance
concepts.description: The variance component in random forests due to randomization conditional on a fixed training set, which increases as the number of randomly selected variables (m) decreases.

concepts.name: random forest as adaptive nearest neighbors
concepts.description: An interpretation of random forests as a weighted k-nearest neighbor classifier where the tree-growing algorithm finds optimal paths to training observations and the averaging process assigns weights forming an equivalent kernel.

concepts.name: forward stagewise linear regression
concepts.description: A regularization algorithm that starts with zero coefficients and at each iteration selects the basis function that best fits current residuals, incrementing its coefficient by an infinitesimal amount while leaving others unchanged.

concepts.name: l1 normalized margin
concepts.description: A margin measure for classification models defined as the minimum over training samples of y·f(x) divided by the L1 norm of coefficients, measuring distance to closest training point in L∞ units.

concepts.name: markov random field
concepts.description: An undirected graphical model where vertices represent random variables and the absence of edges implies conditional independence given other variables, also known as Markov networks.

concepts.name: pairwise markov independence
concepts.description: The property in Markov graphs that if two vertices are not adjacent, the corresponding random variables are conditionally independent given all other variables.

concepts.name: clique potential
concepts.description: Positive functions ψC(·) in the factorization of probability densities over Markov graphs that capture dependence in variables within a clique by scoring certain instances higher than others, though not necessarily being density functions themselves.

concepts.name: hammersley-clifford theorem
concepts.description: A fundamental result stating that for Markov networks with positive distributions, the representation of probability density as a product of clique potentials is equivalent to the independence properties defined by the graph structure.
